Category: --- BUDGET & COST STRUCTURE: the planned allocation of project funds ---

Description: The total approved budget for a project — the financial ceiling against which all costs are measured. Lifecycle: Created (preconstruction) → Approved → Active → Modified (via budget modifications) → Closed (at closeout). Original budget vs. revised budget tracks scope growth. Key properties: total budget amount, original budget, revised budget, contingency amount, allowance amount. Budget = planned spend, NOT actual cost — actual cost comes from commitments, change orders, and direct costs. Budget vs. actual variance is the fundamental financial metric. Downstream: budget line items, budget modifications, cost forecasts.
Industry Standard: AACE 18R-97 (Cost Estimate Classification); PMI PMBOK Cost Management; ASTM E2516 (Cost Classification)
Common Values: $500K–$500M+ depending on project type and delivery method
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300 CRE; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Primavera Unifier; MS Excel; Oracle Aconex
Procore Coverage: High — budget_line_items table, dedicated financial module with wide adoption
Benchmarking Use: Compare budget-to-actual variance across project types and value bands; budget accuracy (original vs. final) by delivery method
Notes: Not all customers purchase financial tools. Projects without budget module should be excluded from cost benchmarks. Budget availability is gated by tool purchase — absence means no tool, not no budget.

Description: An individual row in the project budget — allocates a specific dollar amount to a specific cost code or scope of work. Lifecycle: Created → Active → Modified (via budget modifications and change orders) → Closed. Key properties: amount (original), amount (revised), cost code, WBS code, budget code, committed cost, direct cost, projected cost, variance. Budget line item amount = PLANNED allocation, NOT actual cost. Actual cost = committed cost + direct cost + approved changes. Variance = budget amount − (committed + direct + approved changes). Projected cost includes pending changes. Every financial transaction ties back to a budget line item via cost code.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat (cost code basis); company-specific chart of accounts
Common Values: 10–500+ line items per project depending on complexity
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300 CRE; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Primavera; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — budget_line_items table, foundational to all financial tracking
Benchmarking Use: Budget line item variance by cost code division enables cross-project cost comparison at the building-system level
Notes: Budget line items are the bridge between money and work. Maps to building systems through cost code / WBS code — a budget line for '05 12 00 Structural Steel' maps to the Structural Frame system.

Description: A categorization code that classifies costs by type of work — typically based on MasterFormat divisions. The Rosetta Stone between financial data and building systems. Key properties: code number, description, MasterFormat division, standard vs. custom, project-specific or company-standard. Division 03 = Concrete → Foundations + Structural Frame. Division 26 = Electrical → Electrical system. Company-specific codes must normalize to MasterFormat for cross-company benchmarking.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat (50 divisions); UniFormat; company-specific codes
Common Values: 02 Sitework, 03 Concrete, 05 Steel, 09 Finishes, 15 Mechanical, 26 Electrical
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation Software; Spectrum; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — cost_codes table, foundational classification system
Benchmarking Use: Normalize company codes to MasterFormat for cross-company cost comparison by division and building system
Notes: Cost codes vary wildly between companies. Some use 3-level (division.section.subsection), others use flat codes. UCDM must normalize to MasterFormat for comparability.

Description: Work Breakdown Structure code that adds a second dimension to cost classification — segments cost within a cost code by phase, location, or category. Example: cost code 03 30 00 (Cast-in-Place Concrete) + WBS 'Foundation' vs. WBS 'Elevated Slab'. Enables building-element-level cost tracking within a single cost code. WBS 'Rework' or 'Warranty' segments are signals for rework cost detection. Downstream: all financial line items that reference WBS.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK WBS (§5.4); MIL-STD-881 (defense WBS)
Common Values: Phase-based, location-based, system-based, or hybrid segmentation
Source Systems: Procore; Primavera P6 (native WBS); MS Project; CMiC; Viewpoint
Procore Coverage: Medium — exists within budget structure, adoption varies widely across companies
Benchmarking Use: WBS-level cost comparison enables system-specific and location-specific benchmarking within a cost code
Notes: WBS code adoption varies. Some companies use them extensively (5+ segments per cost code), others not at all. Budget codes serve a similar segmentation purpose.

Description: A grouping code applied to budget line items for reporting and categorization — distinct from cost codes. Key properties: code, description, project-specific or company-standard. Budget codes are a REPORTING overlay, not a transactional code. They group budget lines for executive summaries (e.g., 'Structure', 'Enclosure', 'MEP', 'General Conditions', 'Contingency'). Budget codes with values like 'Rework' or 'Warranty' are signals for rework cost detection.
Industry Standard: Company-specific reporting hierarchies; loosely aligned with UniFormat categories
Common Values: Structure, Enclosure, MEP, General Conditions, Contingency, Site, Interiors
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — budget_codes table exists, not all companies use them
Benchmarking Use: Budget code roll-ups enable executive-level cost comparison across projects
Notes: Budget codes are the least standardized of the three code types. Highly company-specific. Useful for roll-up reporting but not for detailed cost analysis. Can map to building system groups — 'Structure' = Foundations + Frame, 'MEP' = HVAC + Plumbing + Electrical.

Category: --- COMMITMENTS: contracted obligations to pay for work ---

Description: A legally binding agreement between the GC and a subcontractor for a defined scope of work — the largest category of project spend. Lifecycle: Draft → Out for Signature → Executed → Active → Complete → Closed. Key properties: vendor, original amount, revised amount (after COs), scope of work, cost code(s), execution date, retainage %, insurance and bond requirements. Original amount + approved CCOs = revised amount. Committed cost on a budget line = sum of commitment line items mapped to that cost code. A single subcontract can span multiple cost codes and building systems. Upstream: bid packages, budget line items. Downstream: commitment line items, CCOs, subcontractor invoices, retainage, lien waivers.
Industry Standard: AIA A101/A201 (owner-contractor); AIA A401 (contractor-sub); ConsensusDocs 750
Common Values: 25–40 subcontracts per project covering 70–85% of project cost
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation; Spectrum
Procore Coverage: High — commitments table, core financial instrument with wide adoption
Benchmarking Use: Compare commitment coverage (% of budget committed) and subcontract count across project types and value bands
Notes: Subcontracts are the primary financial instrument in construction. The vendor on a commitment links the financial domain to the Organizations domain. Mapped to building systems via cost codes on line items.

Description: An agreement to purchase materials, equipment, or services from a supplier — typically for materials not included in a subcontract scope. Lifecycle: Draft → Issued → Active → Complete → Closed. Shorter duration than subcontracts, material-focused, simpler change order workflow. Key properties: vendor, amount, cost code(s), delivery date, terms, freight. PO delivery tracking connects to the Field Operations domain (delivery logs). Upstream: budget line items. Downstream: commitment line items, CCOs, supplier invoices.
Industry Standard: UCC Article 2 (sale of goods); company procurement policies
Common Values: $5K–$500K per PO; 10–30 POs per project depending on self-perform scope
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation; Spectrum; QuickBooks
Procore Coverage: High — same commitments table (type field distinguishes subcontract vs. PO)
Benchmarking Use: Compare PO spend ratio (PO $ / total committed $) across project types and self-perform levels
Notes: POs are tracked in the same commitment system as subcontracts but with simpler workflows. Material PO delays are a primary schedule risk — connects to the Material Shortage insight.

Description: A cost-code-level detail row within a commitment — breaks the total commitment into budget-aligned allocations. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, amount, commitment reference. Created with the commitment, modified by CCOs, closed with the commitment. The sum of commitment line items by cost code = committed cost on corresponding budget line items. Line item descriptions are a rich NLP signal for cost categorization. Upstream: parent commitment. Downstream: budget line items (as committed cost), CCO line items.
Industry Standard: Follows parent commitment's standard/format
Common Values: 5–500 line items per commitment depending on scope granularity
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — commitment_line_items table, foundational to financial tracking
Benchmarking Use: Line item granularity enables cost-code-level benchmarking of committed cost across projects
Notes: Line item granularity varies by project and company. More granular = better cost tracking but more administrative burden. Direct cost-code-to-building-system mapping at the line item level.

Category: --- CHANGE MANAGEMENT: how scope and cost changes flow through the system ---

Description: Earliest financial signal of scope/cost change. change_reason values: design_development, owner_directive, unforeseen_conditions, value_engineering, scope_change, rework, allowance, regulatory, escalation. CRITICAL: CE and CCO track the SAME change — NEVER sum, use GREATEST(CE, CCO). Void CEs (mapped_to_status = 'void') must be excluded. origin_type links to triggering document (RFI, observation). Lifecycle: Created → Pending → Approved → Void. Key properties: title, description, change_reason (structured enum), origin_type, cost amount (estimated), schedule impact. change_reason is the PRIMARY categorization field — NOT origin_id (which is a bigint FK, 93% null). Upstream: RFIs, observations, owner directives, differing conditions. Downstream: CE line items, CPCOs.
Industry Standard: AACE RP 10S-90 (Cost Engineering); AIA G701 (Change Order); ConsensusDocs 704
Common Values: $1K–$5M per CE; 50–500+ CEs per project depending on complexity
Source Systems: Procore; Primavera Unifier; CMiC; Viewpoint; Newforma; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — change_events table with structured change_reason field, dedicated change management module
Benchmarking Use: Compare change event volume and cost by change_reason category across project types; design change % as share of total CEs
Notes: CEs are the earliest financial signal of a change. 6,748 companies have CEs with design-related change_reason. CE origin_type links back to the triggering document (RFI, observation). change_reason is the PRIMARY categorization field — NOT origin_id (which is a bigint FK, 93% null).

Description: A cost-code-level detail row within a change event — breaks the estimated change cost into budget-aligned allocations. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, latest_cost_amount (current estimate, not original), commitment_potential_change_order_line_item_id (link to CPCO). Created with CE, updated as estimates refine, resolved when CPCO/CCO is approved. 40.8% have a CPCO line item ID — the most reliable CE→CCO tracing path. Descriptions are a rich NLP signal for cost categorization (rework, design changes) even when change_reason is null.
Industry Standard: Follows parent CE standard/format
Common Values: 1–50 line items per CE depending on scope breadth
Source Systems: Procore; Primavera Unifier; CMiC; Viewpoint
Procore Coverage: High — change_event_line_items table with cost code and WBS detail
Benchmarking Use: Line-item-level change cost analysis by cost code enables building-system-specific change tracking
Notes: WBS codes on CE line items can identify specific elements (e.g., WBS 'Rework' within cost code '09 29 00 Gypsum Board'). latest_cost_amount is the current estimate — use this for cost totals.

Description: An intermediate instrument that bridges a change event to a commitment change order — represents the GC's request to modify a specific subcontract based on an approved change. Lifecycle: Created (from CE) → Pending → Approved (becomes CCO) → Void. Key properties: title, status, cost amount, commitment reference, change event reference, commitment_change_order_id. CPCOs exist because a single change event may affect multiple subcontracts — one design change might create CPCOs for structural, mechanical, and electrical subs. CPCO→CCO link is 89.5% populated. CPCO line items table is NOT in the gold data layer.
Industry Standard: AIA G709 (Proposal Request); company-specific RFP/CPCO workflows
Common Values: 1–5 CPCOs per CE depending on number of affected subs
Source Systems: Procore; Primavera Unifier; CMiC; Viewpoint
Procore Coverage: High — commitment_potential_change_orders table, bridges CE to CCO in change workflow
Benchmarking Use: Track CPCO-to-CCO conversion rate and timeline across projects
Notes: CPCOs are where changes get allocated to specific vendors. The CPCO is the bridge: CE identifies → CPCO formalizes against a commitment → CCO executes. Inherits building system mapping from parent CE.

Description: Formal modification to a subcontract/PO that changes committed dollar amount. change_reason values (title case): Design Development, Owner Directive, Unforeseen Conditions, Value Engineering, Scope Change, Rework. Status: 96.7% Approved. grand_total is the cost column. CCOs have INDEPENDENT change_reason — not inherited from CE. Always use LOWER() for CE vs. CCO comparison (CE snake_case, CCO title case). CCOs can exist WITHOUT a corresponding CE. Lifecycle: Draft → Pending → Approved → Void; also Proceeding. 250K design CCOs, 4,200 cos, $13.8B committed cost. Upstream: CPCOs, or created directly. Downstream: commitment line items (modifies amounts), sub invoices (billable after approval), budget line items (updates committed cost).
Industry Standard: AIA G701 (Change Order); ConsensusDocs 704; company-specific CO forms
Common Values: $500–$2M per CCO; CCOs are 6.4x larger than corresponding CEs on average
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation; Spectrum
Procore Coverage: High — commitment_change_orders table with structured change_reason, dedicated module
Benchmarking Use: Compare CCO volume and cost by change_reason; committed change cost as % of original commitment
Notes: 250K CCOs with design-related change_reason across 4,200 companies. $1.45B saved from CE/CCO deduplication in rework analysis. CCO grand_total is the cost column.

Description: A cost-code-level detail row within a commitment change order — modifies specific commitment line item amounts. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, extended_amount (line item cost), commitment_potential_change_order_id (100% populated — reliable link to CPCO). Created and approved with parent CCO. Line item descriptions are NLP signal for cost categorization. Direct wbs_code_id enables building-element-level cost attribution for change orders.
Industry Standard: Follows parent CCO standard/format
Common Values: 1–50 line items per CCO depending on scope breadth
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — commitment_change_order_line_items table with WBS and cost code detail
Benchmarking Use: CCO line item analysis by cost code enables system-specific change cost benchmarking
Notes: CCO line items are where committed cost changes actually land in the budget. Sum of CCO line items by cost code = net change to committed cost on corresponding budget lines.

Description: A formal modification to the prime contract between owner and GC — changes the contract value and potentially the schedule. Lifecycle: Draft → Pending → Approved → Void. Key properties: title, description, status, amount, schedule impact, prime contract reference. Distinct from commitment COs — owner COs change GC revenue, not subcontract cost. A single owner CO may aggregate multiple change events. The delta between owner CO amounts and commitment CO amounts is where GC margin on changes lives. Upstream: change events (aggregated), owner directives. Downstream: prime contract (modifies value), owner invoices (billable), budget modifications.
Industry Standard: AIA G701 (Change Order); ConsensusDocs 704; owner-specific CO forms
Common Values: $5K–$10M per owner CO; fewer but larger than commitment COs
Source Systems: Procore; Primavera Unifier; CMiC; Viewpoint; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — prime_contract_change_orders table exists, lower adoption than commitment-side change orders
Benchmarking Use: Compare owner CO markup (owner CO amount vs. commitment CO cost) to benchmark GC margin on changes
Notes: Not all projects track owner COs in construction software. Owner CO timing vs. commitment CO timing reveals cash flow risk — GC may commit to sub COs before owner approves the funding.

Description: REWORK SIGNAL: Modifications to rework-coded budget lines signal actual rework exceeding planned budget — 339 cos. Internal transfer between budget line items (moves money, does not change total budget). transfer_amount (NOT 'amount'), notes (NOT 'description'), direct wbs_code_id. Lifecycle: Created → Approved → Posted. The transfer amount is a budget adjustment, not a standalone cost transaction. Maps to building systems via cost codes on source and destination budget lines. Upstream/downstream: budget line items (source and destination).
Industry Standard: Company-specific budget transfer policies; AACE cost control practices
Common Values: $1K–$1M per modification; 10–100+ per project
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — budget_modifications table with direct WBS code linkage
Benchmarking Use: Budget modification patterns reveal where cost overruns are occurring — transfers FROM contingency TO specific codes indicate overrun locations
Notes: 339 companies have rework-coded budget modifications. Maps to building systems via cost codes on source and destination budget line items. transfer_amount is the correct field (not 'amount'); notes is the correct field (not 'description').

Description: A non-commitment expenditure — costs that don't flow through a subcontract or purchase order. Includes GC self-performed labor, small material purchases, equipment rental, T&M charges, reimbursables. Lifecycle: Created → Approved → Paid. Key properties: vendor (optional), description, amount, cost code, WBS code, date, invoice number. Direct costs charge against budget line items but bypass the commitment/invoice workflow. Upstream: budget line items (budgeted amount). Downstream: direct cost line items, budget line items (as actual direct cost).
Industry Standard: Company-specific cost tracking policies; AACE cost classification
Common Values: $100–$500K per direct cost entry; represents 15–30% of project cost depending on self-perform scope
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation; QuickBooks; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — direct_costs table, dedicated tracking for non-commitment expenses
Benchmarking Use: Compare direct cost ratio (DC $ / total project cost) across project types and self-perform levels
Notes: Companies that self-perform more have higher direct cost ratios and lower commitment ratios. Direct costs are the 'other' cost bucket — everything not covered by subcontracts or POs.

Description: A cost-code-level detail row within a direct cost — enables granular cost tracking for non-commitment expenses. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, amount, direct cost reference. Provides the same cost-code-level granularity as commitment and change order line items. Description and WBS code are NLP signals for cost categorization. WBS-coded rework direct costs = $399M across the portfolio.
Industry Standard: Follows parent direct cost format
Common Values: 1–20 line items per direct cost entry
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — direct_cost_line_items table with cost code and WBS detail
Benchmarking Use: Direct cost line item analysis by cost code enables non-commitment cost benchmarking at building-system level
Notes: Direct cost line item descriptions contain actionable keywords — 'rework', 'repair', 'correction', 'redo'. $500M in rework-related direct costs identified via NLP on line item descriptions.

Category: --- BILLING & PAYMENT: how money moves between parties ---

Description: A periodic billing from the GC to the owner for work completed — the GC's request for payment under the prime contract. Lifecycle: Draft → Submitted → Under Review → Approved → Paid; also Rejected, Revised. Key properties: billing period, scheduled value, work completed (previous + current), stored materials, retainage, amount due, percent complete. Pay app amount ≠ project cost — it's the billing. Percent complete by line item based on schedule of values. Stored materials (on site but not installed) are billable on many contracts. Retainage (5–10%) withheld until substantial completion. Upstream: commitments, direct costs, owner COs. Downstream: payment, retainage.
Industry Standard: AIA G702/G703 (Application and Certificate for Payment); ConsensusDocs payment forms
Common Values: Monthly billing cycle; $50K–$10M per pay application depending on project size
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — owner_invoices table with line items, markups, and workflow tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare pay application processing time (submission to approval) and billing accuracy across projects
Notes: Monthly pay application cycle drives the financial rhythm of every project. Lender draws are often tied to pay app approval. Pay app line items correspond to SOV items mapping to cost codes.

Description: A periodic billing from a subcontractor to the GC for work completed under a subcontract. Lifecycle: Draft → Submitted → Under Review → Approved → Paid; also Rejected, Revised. Key properties: billing period, commitment reference, scheduled value, work completed, stored materials, retainage, amount due, percent complete. Mirrors pay applications but flows in the opposite direction — subs bill the GC. Invoice review and approval drives cash flow for subcontractors. Upstream: commitments, CCOs (approved changes added to SOV). Downstream: payment, retainage, lien waiver (required with each invoice).
Industry Standard: AIA G702/G703 (adapted for sub billing); company-specific invoice forms
Common Values: Monthly billing; 25–40 subs × 12–24 months = 300–960 invoices per project
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation
Procore Coverage: High — subcontractor_invoices table with workflow tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare invoice processing time and rejection rate across vendors and projects
Notes: Subcontractor invoice volume is high. Matching invoice amounts to approved work is a major administrative task. Invoice processing time is a key vendor relationship metric.

Description: Funds withheld from progress payments as security for completion of work — released at substantial completion or final completion. Lifecycle: Accumulating (during construction) → Partial Release (at substantial completion) → Full Release (at final completion). Key properties: retainage held (cumulative), retainage released, retainage rate (%), release conditions. Retainage is NOT a cost — it's a withheld portion of earned payment. Typical rate: 5–10%. Release triggers: substantial completion (reduce to 50%), final completion (release remainder). Retainage held from subs should approximately equal retainage held from owner. Upstream: owner invoices, subcontractor invoices. Downstream: payment, lien waiver.
Industry Standard: State retainage statutes (vary by jurisdiction); AIA contract retainage provisions; Miller Act (federal projects)
Common Values: 5–10% of each progress payment; total retainage = 5–10% of contract value
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked within invoicing system, no dedicated retainage table
Benchmarking Use: Compare retainage rates and release timing across project types and jurisdictions
Notes: Retainage is one of the most contentious financial instruments in construction. Subs view it as the GC holding their money. Some states cap retainage at 5%. Release delays strain vendor relationships.

Description: The actual transfer of funds between parties — the final step in the billing cycle. Lifecycle: Scheduled → Processed → Cleared. Key properties: amount, date, from party, to party, invoice reference, payment method. Payment is the actual cash event — distinct from invoice approval. Payment terms (Net 30, Net 45) create a gap between approval and payment. Cash flow forecasting depends on payment timing. Pay-when-paid clauses mean sub payment depends on owner payment to GC. Upstream: owner invoices, subcontractor invoices, retainage release. Downstream: lien waiver.
Industry Standard: Prompt Payment Acts (federal and state); UCC payment provisions; contract-specific terms
Common Values: Net 30 to Net 60 payment terms; $10K–$5M per payment
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; bank EFT systems
Procore Coverage: High — payments_* tables (agreements, disbursements, issued, received), comprehensive payment tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare payment cycle time (invoice approval to payment) and prompt payment compliance across projects
Notes: Payment timing drives cash flow. Slow-paying owners cascade delays to sub payments. Construction lenders tie draws to pay app approval, adding another layer.

Description: A legal document in which a party waives their right to file a mechanic's lien against the property — exchanged with each payment. Four types: conditional progress (with invoice), unconditional progress (when payment clears), conditional final (with final invoice), unconditional final (with final payment). Collection from all tiers of subcontractors required before owner makes final payment to GC. Upstream: payment. Downstream: none — terminal document.
Industry Standard: State mechanic's lien statutes (vary by jurisdiction); statutory lien waiver forms in many states
Common Values: 4 types × number of subs × number of billing periods = hundreds per project
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Levelset; zlien; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — payments_lien_waivers table with subtier tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare lien waiver collection rate and timeliness across projects and vendors
Notes: Lien waiver collection is a major closeout task. Missing lien waivers can block final payment and retainage release. Some states have statutory lien waiver forms that must be used exactly.

Category: --- FINANCIAL CLASSIFICATION & STRUCTURE: how costs are organized and categorized ---

Description: A cost breakdown of a contract that defines the billing structure — line items represent discrete portions of work that can be billed as completed. Lifecycle: Created (with contract) → Active (updated each billing cycle) → Complete (100% billed). Key properties: line item number, description, scheduled value, percent complete, previous billing, current billing, balance to finish. The SOV is a BILLING structure, not a cost structure — SOV line items may not align with cost code line items. Percent complete by SOV line drives monthly billing. Front-loading (billing more early) is a common practice that distorts true percent complete. Upstream: commitments. Downstream: subcontractor invoices, owner invoices.
Industry Standard: AIA G703 (Continuation Sheet); company-specific SOV formats
Common Values: 10–500 SOV line items per contract depending on scope complexity
Source Systems: Procore; Textura; GCPay; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — embedded in commitment and billing structure, no dedicated SOV table
Benchmarking Use: Compare SOV granularity and front-loading patterns across project types
Notes: SOV is one of the least standardized instruments. SOV lines often describe building elements in plain language ('Install curtain wall Level 3-5'), providing NLP-accessible links to building systems.

Description: A budget line item for work that cannot be precisely defined at contract time — price is estimated and reconciled to actual cost. Lifecycle: Established (in contract) → Active (as work performed) → Reconciled (actual vs. allowance). Key properties: description, allowance amount, actual cost, variance (allowance − actual), cost code. Allowances are NOT contingency — they are placeholder amounts for defined scope where exact cost isn't yet known (e.g., 'Flooring Allowance: $50/SF, final selection TBD'). Exceeded allowances become change orders. Upstream: budget line items, commitments. Downstream: direct costs or CCOs, budget modifications.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §3.8 (Allowances); owner specifications
Common Values: $10K–$2M per allowance; 3–15 allowances per project on negotiated contracts
Source Systems: Procore (via budget codes); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated allowance tracking, managed via budget codes and manual processes
Benchmarking Use: Compare allowance variance (actual vs. allowed) by type to benchmark estimation accuracy
Notes: Allowances are common in negotiated contracts (CM at Risk, design-build) and less common in hard bid. Typically tied to specific building systems — flooring, fixtures, lighting.

Description: A budget reserve for unforeseen costs — not allocated to any specific scope until needed. Lifecycle: Established (in budget) → Active (drawn down as changes occur) → Depleted or Returned. Key properties: contingency type (owner vs. GC vs. design), original amount, remaining amount, burn rate, drawdown log. Contingency is NOT a cost — it's a RESERVE. Contingency burn rate (% used vs. % complete) is a key project health metric. Contingency remaining at completion may be shared (GMP savings) or returned to owner. Upstream: project budget. Downstream: budget modifications, change orders (funded from contingency).
Industry Standard: AACE 10S-90 (Cost Engineering); PMI PMBOK contingency reserves; CMAA guidelines
Common Values: 5–15% of construction cost depending on project complexity and delivery method
Source Systems: Procore (via budget line items); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Primavera Unifier; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated contingency tracking, managed via budget line items and budget codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare contingency burn rate across project types and delivery methods to benchmark risk management
Notes: Types: owner contingency (owner controls), GC contingency (GC controls), design contingency (for design-phase changes). GMP contracts define contingency explicitly. Burn rate > % complete = red flag.

Category: --- EARNED VALUE & FORECASTING: derived financial measures computed from transactional instruments ---

Description: A projection of total project cost at completion — combines actual cost to date with estimated cost to complete. Updated monthly or more frequently on active projects. EAC = Actual Cost to Date + Estimated Cost to Complete. Methods: deterministic (bottom-up re-estimate), parametric (CPI-based), management estimate. EAC vs. budget = projected variance at completion — THE financial health metric. Can be computed at any level: project, building system, cost code, commitment. Depends on all other financial instruments — budget, commitments, changes, direct costs, invoices.
Industry Standard: AACE RP 10S-90; PMI PMBOK §7.4 (Control Costs); ANSI/EIA-748 (EVMS)
Common Values: Computed from: original budget + committed cost + actual cost + projected changes + cost to complete
Source Systems: Procore (budget forecast snapshots); Primavera; CMiC; Viewpoint; Sage; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — budget_forecast_line_items table exists but sparse adoption; most EAC done in Excel
Benchmarking Use: Compare EAC accuracy (projected vs. actual at completion) and forecast frequency across project types
Notes: EAC is computed, not stored as a standalone instrument. Included here because it is the most important derived financial measure. System-level EAC shows which building systems are trending over/under budget.

Description: A time-phased projection of money in (owner payments) and money out (sub payments, direct costs) — shows when cash is needed. Updated monthly. Key properties: period, projected income, projected outflow, net cash position, cumulative cash position. GCs typically pay subs after receiving owner payment (pay-when-paid). Positive cash flow depends on billing ahead of payment obligations. Front-loaded SOVs improve early cash flow. Retainage creates a cash drag until release. Can be projected by building system — envelope peaks during enclosure phase, MEP peaks during rough-in.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK cash flow management; AACE cost scheduling
Common Values: Monthly or weekly time buckets; S-curve distribution typical
Source Systems: Procore (limited); Primavera P6 (resource-loaded schedule); CMiC; MS Excel (primary tool)
Procore Coverage: Low — not a native Procore feature, most GCs manage cash flow in Excel or ERP
Benchmarking Use: Compare cash flow profiles (S-curve shape) and peak cash requirements across project types and sizes
Notes: Cash flow management is critical for GC financial health. Negative cash flow on a project means the GC is financing construction from other projects or credit lines. Connects Financial Instruments to the Schedule domain.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from financial instrument performance ---

Description: The difference between budgeted cost and actual cost (committed + direct + approved changes) at any point in time. Formula: Budget Amount − (Committed Cost + Direct Cost + Approved Changes). Can be computed per cost code, per building system, or at project level. Negative variance = over budget. The most fundamental financial KPI in construction.
Industry Standard: AACE cost variance analysis; PMI PMBOK EVM
Common Values: Expressed as $ amount and as % of budget; typical range: −15% to +5%
Source Systems: Derived from: budget_line_items, commitment_line_items, direct_cost_line_items, change_event_line_items
Procore Coverage: High — computed from existing financial tables
Benchmarking Use: Compare budget variance by cost code division and project type to identify systematic cost risks
Notes: Variance by MasterFormat division enables cross-project comparison — 'are my concrete costs consistently over budget?' Budget variance at completion is the ultimate project financial outcome.

Description: The percentage increase from original budget to final cost. Formula: (Final Cost − Original Budget) / Original Budget × 100. Measures total cost escalation including all changes, direct costs, and adjustments. Distinct from change order rate — cost growth includes ALL cost increases, not just formal changes.
Industry Standard: AACE 10S-90; CII benchmarking metrics
Common Values: Typical range: 3–12% for well-managed projects; 15–30% for troubled projects
Source Systems: Derived from: budget (original), all cost instruments (final)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from budget and cost data
Benchmarking Use: Compare cost growth across project types
Notes: delivery methods
column10: and value bands to benchmark financial performance
column11: Cost growth is the single most important outcome metric for financial benchmarking. Separating cost growth by change_reason (design, owner, unforeseen) identifies root causes.

Description: The ratio of earned value to actual cost — measures cost efficiency. Formula: CPI = Earned Value / Actual Cost. CPI > 1.0 = under budget. CPI < 1.0 = over budget. CPI = 1.0 = on budget. Used as a multiplier in EAC forecasting. Typically computed monthly.
Industry Standard: ANSI/EIA-748 (EVMS); PMI PMBOK §7.4; AACE RP 10S-90
Common Values: CPI range: 0.70–1.30; below 0.85 is a serious red flag
Source Systems: Derived from: schedule (earned value basis), budget, actual costs
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires earned value computation which most GCs don't formally track
Benchmarking Use: Compare CPI trends across projects and project types to identify cost efficiency patterns
Notes: True EVMS is rare in commercial construction — more common on government/DOD projects. Simplified CPI (% complete × budget / actual cost) is more practical for benchmarking.

Description: The ratio of total committed cost (subcontracts + POs + approved changes) to budget. Formula: Total Committed / Budget × 100. Measures how much of the budget is locked into contracts. Tracks the buying-out process — from 0% at project start to 90–100% when all trades are committed.
Industry Standard: AACE cost control; PMI procurement management
Common Values: 0–100%+; typically 85–95% of budget is committed, remainder is direct cost and contingency
Source Systems: Derived from: commitments, commitment_change_orders, budget_line_items
Procore Coverage: High — computed from commitment and budget tables
Benchmarking Use: Compare commitment coverage trajectory (speed of buy-out) and final committed ratio across projects
Notes: Commitment coverage < 70% late in the project = buying risk. Committed cost > budget = budget overrun before direct costs are even counted.

Description: The number and value of change orders relative to original contract value. Formula: (Total CO Value / Original Contract Value) × 100. Can be computed separately for owner COs (revenue) and commitment COs (cost). High CO rate may indicate scope issues, poor design, or difficult conditions — not necessarily bad management.
Industry Standard: CII benchmarking metrics; AACE change management
Common Values: By count: 20–100+ COs per project. By value: 5–15% of contract value is typical
Source Systems: Derived from: change_events, commitment_change_orders, prime_contract_change_orders, commitments
Procore Coverage: High — computed from change management and commitment tables
Benchmarking Use: Compare CO rate by change_reason category across project types and delivery methods
Notes: Separating CO rate by change_reason (design, owner, unforeseen, rework) reveals root causes. Design change rate + owner change rate + unforeseen change rate = total CO rate decomposition.

Description: The rate at which contingency is consumed relative to project progress. Formula: (Contingency Used / Original Contingency) / (% Complete). Burn rate > 1.0 = contingency consumed faster than progress, indicating the project may exhaust reserves. Updated monthly. A leading indicator of financial distress.
Industry Standard: AACE contingency management; CMAA project controls guidelines
Common Values: Burn rate range: 0.5–2.0; > 1.5 is a red flag
Source Systems: Derived from: budget_line_items (contingency lines), budget_modifications (contingency draws), schedule (% complete)
Procore Coverage: Low — requires manual identification of contingency budget lines and draws
Benchmarking Use: Compare contingency burn rate trajectories across project types and complexity levels
Notes: Contingency burn rate is one of the few LEADING financial indicators — it predicts future budget distress before it materializes. Most other financial metrics are lagging.

Description: The elapsed time from invoice submission to approval. Formula: Approval Date − Submission Date (in business days). Measures administrative efficiency and cash flow impact. Long processing times strain subcontractor relationships and signal workflow bottlenecks.
Industry Standard: Prompt Payment Act benchmarks; AIA payment provisions
Common Values: Typical range: 5–30 business days; > 20 days is a red flag for sub relations
Source Systems: Derived from: owner_invoices (workflow dates), subcontractor_invoices (workflow dates)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from invoice and workflow timestamps
Benchmarking Use: Compare invoice processing time across projects and vendors to identify bottlenecks
Notes: Prompt Payment Acts in many states require payment within 30 days of invoice receipt. Invoice processing time directly affects sub cash flow and willingness to bid future work.

Description: The difference between projected cash position and actual cash position at a point in time. Formula: Actual Cash Position − Projected Cash Position. Positive = more cash than expected. Negative = less cash than expected. Tracks the accuracy of cash flow forecasting and identifies liquidity risk.
Industry Standard: AACE cash flow analysis; PMI cost management
Common Values: Expressed as $ amount; typical tolerance: ±5% of monthly cash flow
Source Systems: Derived from: payment records, invoice records, budget forecasts
Procore Coverage: Low — requires cash flow projection capability that most Procore users manage in Excel/ERP
Benchmarking Use: Compare cash flow forecast accuracy across projects to improve financial planning
Notes: Cash flow variance connects to payment timing — owner payment delays create negative variance that cascades to sub payments.

Description: The difference between the projected final cost (EAC) and the original budget. Formula: EAC − Original Budget. Tracks how the projected final cost evolves over time. Early EAC variance is a leading indicator of final cost growth.
Industry Standard: AACE 10S-90; ANSI/EIA-748; PMI PMBOK
Common Values: $ amount; typical trajectory: starts at $0, grows as changes are identified
Source Systems: Derived from: budget_forecast_line_items, budget, all cost instruments
Procore Coverage: Medium — budget_forecast_line_items table exists but sparse
Benchmarking Use: Compare EAC variance trajectory shape across project types to identify early warning patterns
Notes: EAC variance at 25% complete is a strong predictor of final cost growth. Projects with high early EAC variance rarely recover. Track monthly EAC snapshots for trend analysis.
